docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
This project aims to develop a real-time, AI-assisted classroom discussion tool designed for discussion-based university lectures in the Department of Pharmacology. The system combines live speech-to-text transcription ("hearing"), presentation content analysis ("seeing"), and AI-driven prompt generation to support interactive, anonymous student participation during lectures.

Instructors use the platform to create and manage lessons, generate discussion prompts from live lecture content, and collect anonymous student responses via QR code or PIN-based access. Students participate through a lightweight web interface without requiring authentication, ensuring privacy and ease of access. After each session, the system automatically aggregates prompts, responses, transcripts, and analytics for instructor review and export.

The primary quality goals of the system are convenience, accuracy, and security, ensuring minimal disruption during live lectures while maintaining reliable AI output and protecting user data.

docs.title: Project Glossary
docs.text:
• Lesson – A single lecture session created and managed by an instructor.
  • Discussion Prompt – A question generated by AI or manually created by an instructor to initiate student discussion.
  • Session – A live instance of a lesson during which students can join and participate.
  • STT (Speech-to-Text) – Real-time transcription of instructor audio during a lecture.
  • QR Code / PIN – Mechanisms used by students to join a live lesson.
  • Anonymous Response – A student submission that is not linked to any identifiable user data.
  • Prompt State – The current lifecycle state of a discussion (e.g., open or closed).
  • Context Builder – A system layer that controls what lecture content is sent to the AI to reduce hallucinations and cost.

docs.title: US 1.01 – Create Account (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to create an account on the platform, so that I can access the system and manage my teaching materials.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor without an account WHEN they click "Sign in with Google" and select their UAlberta email THEN they are redirected to UAlberta SSO login

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they complete UAlberta SSO authentication successfully THEN their account is automatically created and they are logged into the system

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they attempt to sign in with a non-UAlberta Google account THEN authentication is blocked with error message "Please use your UAlberta email"

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they sign in with UAlberta email for the first time THEN they are directed to the instructor dashboard

docs.title: US 1.02 – Login via UAlberta Authentication (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to login via UAlberta authentication, so that I can access my materials and have instructor privileges.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with existing account WHEN they click "Sign in with Google" and authenticate via UAlberta SSO THEN they successfully login and access the instructor dashboard

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they fail UAlberta SSO authentication THEN login fails and they see error message "Authentication failed"

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they close and reopen the application within session timeout THEN they remain logged in without re-authenticating

docs.title: US 1.03 – Logout Securely (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to logout securely, so that I can protect my account when finished.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN a logged-in instructor WHEN they click "Logout" from the profile page THEN they are logged out and session is terminated

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they logout THEN they are redirected to the login page

  3. GIVEN a logged-out instructor WHEN they attempt to access protected pages via URL THEN they are redirected to login page

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they logout THEN all session data is cleared from the browser

docs.title: US 1.04 – View Only Own Past Lessons (Could Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want only I to be able to view my past lessons and details, so that my lessons remain private.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they access their dashboard THEN they see only their own past lessons, not other instructors' lessons

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they attempt to access another instructor's lesson via direct URL THEN access is denied with 403 Forbidden error

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view lesson details THEN only data from their own lessons is displayed

docs.title: US 1.05 – Create New Lesson (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to create a new lesson, so that I can prepare content for a lecture.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they click "Create Lesson" and provide a title THEN a new lesson is created with inactive status

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they create a lesson without providing a title THEN the system generates a default title with current date/time

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they create a new lesson THEN it appears in their lesson list

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they create a lesson without uploading files THEN the lesson is still created successfully

docs.title: US 1.06 – Start a Lesson (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to start a lesson, so that students can join and I can begin teaching.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with a created lesson WHEN they click "Start Lesson" THEN the lesson status changes to active

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they start a lesson THEN a unique PIN code is generated and displayed

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they start a lesson THEN a unique QR code is generated and displayed

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN the lesson is active THEN students can join using the PIN or QR code

docs.title: US 1.07 – Cancel a Lesson (Won't Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to cancel a lesson, so that I can abort lesson creation if needed.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor creating a lesson WHEN they click "Cancel" THEN the lesson is not saved to their lesson list

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they cancel lesson creation THEN they are returned to the appropriate page

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they cancel a lesson THEN any uploaded files for that lesson are discarded

docs.title: US 1.08 – Delete Past Lesson (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to delete a past lesson, so that I can remove outdated or incorrect lessons.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select a past lesson and click "Delete" THEN a confirmation dialog appears

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they confirm deletion THEN the lesson is permanently removed from the system

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they delete a lesson THEN it no longer appears in their lesson list

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they delete a lesson THEN all associated data (prompts, responses, transcripts, files) is permanently deleted

docs.title: US 1.09 – End/Leave a Lesson (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to end/leave a lesson, so that the lesson is closed.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with active lesson WHEN they click "End/Leave Lesson" THEN the lesson status changes to inactive

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end/leave a lesson THEN students cannot submit new responses

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end/leave a lesson THEN students are notified that the lesson has ended

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end/leave a lesson THEN all lesson data (prompts, responses, transcripts) is saved automatically

  5. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end/leave a lesson THEN files, prompts, questions, and analytics are ready for export

docs.title: US 1.10 – Automatic Lesson Save on End (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want the lesson to be saved automatically when I end it, so that transcripts, prompts, and responses are preserved.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end a lesson THEN all lesson data is automatically saved without manual action required

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end a lesson THEN all discussion prompts with timestamps are preserved

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end a lesson THEN all student responses with submission times are preserved

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end a lesson and STT was used THEN all transcripts are preserved

  5. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they access a saved lesson THEN they can view all preserved data

docs.title: US 1.11 – Resume Unfinished Lesson (Could Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to resume a previously unfinished lesson, so that I can continue where I left off.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view their dashboard THEN they can see a list of unfinished lessons separate from finished lessons

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select an unfinished lesson and click "Resume" THEN the lesson opens with all previous data intact

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they resume a lesson THEN previously uploaded files are available

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they resume a lesson THEN previously created discussions are available

docs.title: US 1.12 – Reconnect After Connection Failure (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to reconnect if my connection fails, so that I can continue my lesson where I left off.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with active lesson WHEN connection is lost THEN they can click "Reconnect" to rejoin

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they reconnect to active lesson THEN the lesson state (current discussion, student count) is preserved

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they reconnect THEN student responses submitted during disconnection are visible

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they reconnect THEN they see a message confirming successful reconnection

docs.title: US 1.13 – Automatic Data Saving at Regular Intervals (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to have my lesson data automatically saved at regular intervals, so that progress is not lost if something goes wrong.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with active lesson WHEN they are teaching THEN lesson data is automatically saved at regular intervals

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN connection is lost THEN discussion prompts created before disconnection are preserved

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN connection is lost THEN student responses submitted before disconnection are preserved

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they reconnect after unexpected disconnection THEN auto-saved data is recoverable

docs.title: US 1.14 – View Past Lessons and Details (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to view past lessons and its details, so that I can review transcripts, prompts, and responses.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they access their dashboard THEN they see a list of all past lessons

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select a past lesson THEN they can view the lesson details page

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN viewing past lesson details THEN they see all discussion prompts that were published

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN viewing past lesson details THEN they see all student responses organized by prompt

  5. GIVEN an instructor WHEN viewing past lesson details and STT was used THEN they see all transcripts

docs.title: US 1.15 – Helpful Error Messages (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to see helpful error messages when something goes wrong, so that I can troubleshoot issues.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN file upload fails THEN they see a clear error message indicating the failure reason

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN AI prompt generation fails THEN they see error message "Prompt generation failed. Please try again or enter manually"

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN connection is lost THEN they see error message "Connection lost. Attempting to reconnect..."

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN an error occurs THEN the error message is displayed prominently and clearly

docs.title: US 1.49 – Add Course to Courselist (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to add a course which contains all lessons specific to that course to the courselist, so that I can organize my lessons by course.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they click "Create Course" THEN they can enter a course name and optional description

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they create a course THEN it appears in their courses list

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they create or edit a lesson THEN they can assign it to an existing course

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view a course THEN they see all lessons assigned to that course

docs.title: US 1.50 – Delete Course from Courselist (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to delete a course which contains all the lessons specific to that course to the courselist, so that I can remove courses I no longer need.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select a course and click "Delete" THEN a confirmation dialog appears warning about lesson deletion

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they confirm course deletion THEN the course and all its lessons are permanently removed

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they confirm course deletion THEN all files and metadata associated with lessons in that course are permanently deleted

docs.title: US 1.16 – Upload Files (PDF, PPTX) (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to upload files (PDF, PPTX), so that I can provide sources for discussion prompts.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they upload a PDF file within size limit THEN the file is accepted and appears in the uploaded files list

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they upload a PPTX file within size limit THEN the file is accepted and appears in the uploaded files list

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they attempt to upload an unsupported file type (e.g., .docx, .txt) THEN upload fails with error message "Unsupported file type. Please upload PDF or PPTX"

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they upload files THEN they can see a list of all uploaded files with names and types

  5. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they upload a file THEN the system accepts only 1 file per lesson

docs.title: US 1.17 – Activate/Deactivate STT Transcript Capture (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to activate and deactivate STT transcript capture, so that I can control when my spoken content is recorded.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with active lesson WHEN they click "Start Recording" THEN the recording period begins and STT transcript capture activates

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN recording period is active THEN their speech is transcribed in real-time

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they click "Stop Recording" THEN the recording period ends and STT transcript capture deactivates

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they stop recording THEN the transcript is sent to AI for prompt generation

  5. GIVEN an instructor WHEN recording period is active THEN they can toggle STT on and off multiple times during the lesson

docs.title: US 1.18 – Trigger AI Prompt Generation (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to trigger AI prompt generation, so that the system creates discussion questions based on my content.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with uploaded files WHEN they click "Generate Prompts" THEN AI processes the files and generates discussion prompts

  2. GIVEN an instructor with completed transcript WHEN recording period ends THEN AI automatically generates prompts from the transcript and files

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN AI is generating prompts THEN they see a loading indicator

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN AI completes generation THEN at least 3 discussion prompts are displayed

docs.title: US 1.19 – Review and Select AI Generated Prompts (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to review and select AI generated prompts, so that I can choose the most appropriate discussion question.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN AI generates prompts THEN all generated prompts are displayed for review

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view generated prompts THEN they can select one prompt from the options

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select a prompt THEN it is highlighted or visually marked

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select a prompt THEN they can change their selection before publishing

docs.title: US 1.20 – Edit AI-Generated Prompts (Could Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to edit AI-generated prompts before publishing, so that I can refine the wording.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with selected AI prompt WHEN they click "Edit" THEN the prompt text becomes editable

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they modify the prompt text THEN the changes are saved

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they edit a prompt THEN they can see both original and edited versions

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they edit a prompt THEN they can revert to the original AI-generated text

docs.title: US 1.21 – Manually Enter Prompt (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want the option to manually enter a prompt, so that I can continue the discussion even if AI fails.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they click "Create Custom Prompt" THEN a text input field appears for manual entry

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they manually enter a prompt THEN it functions the same as AI-generated prompts

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they create a manual prompt THEN they can publish it without triggering AI generation

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN AI generation fails THEN they see option to manually enter a prompt

docs.title: US 1.22 – Set AI Prompt Generation Preferences (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to set preferences for how AI generates prompts, so that prompts align with my teaching style.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they access AI settings THEN they can configure prompt generation preferences

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they set preferences THEN AI uses these preferences in future prompt generations

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they change preferences THEN the new settings are saved and applied to subsequent generations

docs.title: US 1.23 – Generate Multiple Choice, Short Answer, and Long Answer Prompts (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to generate multiple choice, short answer, and long answer type discussion prompts, so that I can vary discussion formats.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they trigger AI generation THEN they can specify prompt type (multiple choice, short answer, or long answer)

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select multiple choice format THEN AI generates a question with answer options

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select short answer or long answer format THEN AI generates an open-ended question

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they manually create a prompt THEN they can choose between multiple choice, short answer, or long answer formats

docs.title: US 1.24 – Regenerate AI Prompts (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to regenerate AI prompts if I'm not satisfied, so that I can get alternative suggestions.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with AI-generated prompts WHEN they click "Regenerate" THEN AI generates a new set of prompts

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they regenerate prompts THEN the previous prompts are replaced with new ones

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they regenerate THEN they see a loading indicator while AI generates new prompts

docs.title: US 1.25 – Create Multiple Discussions Within One Lesson (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to create multiple discussions within one lesson, so that I can facilitate ongoing engagement throughout my lecture.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with active lesson WHEN they publish a discussion prompt THEN they can create additional discussions in the same lesson

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they create multiple discussions THEN each discussion is tracked separately

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view lesson data THEN they see all discussions that occurred during the lesson

docs.title: US 1.26 – Private Discussion Prompts per Lesson (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want only students in my lesson to be able to see my discussion prompts, so that discussions remain private to my lesson.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with active lesson WHEN they publish a discussion prompt THEN only students who joined via that lesson's PIN/QR code can see the prompt

  2. GIVEN students in different lessons WHEN an instructor publishes a prompt THEN students only see prompts from their own lesson

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they publish a prompt THEN it is not visible to students in other instructors' lessons

docs.title: US 1.27 – Display Discussion Prompt (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to display the discussion prompt, so that students can see the question.

Points assigned: 1

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with selected/created prompt WHEN they click "Publish" or "Display" THEN the prompt is sent to all connected students

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they display a prompt THEN students see it on their devices within 2 seconds

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they display a prompt THEN it appears in the lesson's discussion history

docs.title: US 1.28 – Start and Close Discussions (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to start and close discussions within a lesson, so that I can control when students can respond.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they publish a discussion prompt THEN the response window opens and students can submit responses

  2. GIVEN an instructor with open discussion WHEN they click "Close Discussion" THEN the response window closes and students can no longer submit

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they close a discussion THEN students see a message indicating the discussion is closed

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they close a discussion THEN they can start a new discussion

docs.title: US 1.29 – Set Time Limit for Response Window (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to set a time limit for a response window, so that I can control how long students have to submit their answers.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor creating a discussion WHEN they enable time limit THEN they can specify duration in minutes and seconds

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they set a time limit THEN students see a countdown timer on their devices

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN the time limit expires THEN the response window automatically closes and students cannot submit

  4. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they publish discussion without time limit THEN students can respond until instructor manually closes the discussion

docs.title: US 1.30 – Configure Single or Multiple Responses (Could Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to configure whether students can submit one or multiple responses per discussion, so that I can control participation based on the type of question.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor creating a discussion WHEN they configure response settings THEN they can choose "Single Response" or "Multiple Responses"

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select "Single Response" THEN each student can only submit one response to that discussion

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they select "Multiple Responses" THEN students can submit multiple responses to that discussion

docs.title: US 1.31 – Display PIN Code (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to display a PIN code for students to join the lesson, so that students can access my session.

Points assigned: 1

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they start a lesson THEN a unique PIN code is generated

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN the lesson is active THEN the PIN code is displayed prominently on their screen

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end the lesson THEN the PIN code is invalidated and cannot be used to join

docs.title: US 1.32 – Display QR Code (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to display a QR code for students to join the lesson, so that students can quickly join by scanning.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they start a lesson THEN a unique QR code is generated

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN the lesson is active THEN the QR code is displayed on their screen for students to scan

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they end the lesson THEN the QR code is invalidated and cannot be used to join

docs.title: US 1.33 – See Student Count (Won't Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to see how many students have joined, so that I can gauge attendance.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with active lesson WHEN students join THEN the student count updates in real-time

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view their lesson dashboard THEN they see the number of currently connected students

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN a student leaves THEN the student count decrements

docs.title: US 1.34 – See Anonymous Student Responses in Real-Time (Must Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to see anonymous student responses in real-time, so that I can monitor engagement and understanding.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with open discussion WHEN students submit responses THEN the responses appear in real-time without manual refresh

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view responses THEN each response is displayed anonymously without student identifiers

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN multiple students submit THEN all responses appear in the list as they are submitted

docs.title: US 1.35 – Hide Inappropriate Responses (Could Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to hide inappropriate responses, so that I can moderate discussion content.

Points assigned: 1

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor viewing responses WHEN they select a response and click "Hide" THEN that response is removed from the visible list

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they hide a response THEN it is no longer visible to them or to students (if responses are shared)

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they hide a response THEN the action is logged but the original response data is preserved

docs.title: US 1.36 – Highlight Specific Response (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to highlight a specific response, so that I can emphasize answers during class discussion.

Points assigned: 1

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor viewing responses WHEN they select a response and click "Highlight" THEN that response is visually emphasized

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they highlight a response THEN it remains highlighted until they remove the highlight or close the discussion

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they highlight a response THEN they can highlight additional responses or remove existing highlights

docs.title: US 1.37 – Scroll Through Student Responses (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to scroll through student responses, so that I can review all submissions.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN students submit responses THEN all responses are displayed in a scrollable list

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN there are many responses THEN they can scroll to view all submissions

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they scroll through responses THEN each response remains readable and properly formatted

docs.title: US 1.38 – View Responses as Word Cloud (Could Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to choose to view responses as a word cloud format, so that I can visualize common themes at a glance.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor viewing responses WHEN they select "Word Cloud" view THEN responses are displayed as a word cloud showing common terms

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view word cloud THEN more frequently used words appear larger

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they switch to word cloud view THEN they can switch back to list view

docs.title: US 1.39 – View Responses in List Format (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to choose to view responses in a list format, so that I can read them sequentially.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor viewing responses WHEN they select "List" view THEN all responses are displayed in a sequential list

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view list format THEN each response is displayed with timestamp

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they are in list view THEN they can switch to other view formats (e.g., word cloud)

docs.title: US 1.40 – See Student Response Metrics (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to see metrics of student responses, so that I can view engagement rate and participation statistics.

Points assigned: 5

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with closed discussion WHEN they view analytics THEN they see participation rate (responses submitted / students connected)

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view metrics THEN they see average response time and submission patterns

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view metrics THEN they see response length distribution

docs.title: US 1.41 – Export Responses as File (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to export responses as a file, so that I can access and analyze them outside of the platform.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with lesson data WHEN they click "Export Responses" THEN they can choose export format (CSV, PDF, or JSON)

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they export as CSV THEN the file contains all responses with timestamps in structured format

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they export THEN the file downloads immediately to their device

docs.title: US 1.42 – Export AI Prompts and Responses (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to export AI generated prompts and its responses as a file, so that I can access and analyze them outside of the platform.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with lesson data WHEN they click "Export Prompts and Responses" THEN they can choose export format

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they export THEN the file contains all prompts with their associated responses

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they export THEN the file includes timestamps for prompts and responses

docs.title: US 1.43 – Export Lesson Statistics (Should Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to export appropriate lesson statistics, so that I can access and analyze them outside of the platform.

Points assigned: 2

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with completed lesson WHEN they click "Export Statistics" THEN they can choose what statistics to include

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they export statistics THEN the file includes participation metrics, response counts, and timing data

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they export statistics THEN the file downloads in their chosen format

docs.title: US 1.44 – See Multiple Choice Question Metrics (Could Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to see multiple choice question metrics, so that I can view statistics like answer distribution and correctness rates.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor with multiple choice discussion WHEN they view results THEN they see how many students selected each option

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view MC metrics THEN they see percentage distribution of answers

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they view MC metrics THEN they see which option was marked as correct and the overall correctness rate

docs.title: US 1.45 – Customized UI Graphics (Won't Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to see customized graphics in the UI for instructors, so that the interface is visually appealing.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they login THEN they see instructor-specific UI graphics and branding

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they use desktop THEN graphics display correctly at desktop resolutions

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they use mobile devices THEN graphics are responsive and display correctly

docs.title: US 1.46 – Fine-Tune AI with Trigger Words (Won't Have)
docs.text:
As an instructor, I want to be able to fine-tune the AI prompt using trigger words, so that I can control prompt generation more precisely.

Points assigned: 3

Acceptance Tests:

  1. GIVEN an instructor on AI settings WHEN they add trigger words THEN those words are saved to the lesson or profile settings

  2. GIVEN an instructor WHEN they say the trigger word THEN the AI prompt generation occurs

  3. GIVEN an instructor WHEN no trigger words are configured THEN default AI prompt generation behavior is used

docs.title: Kahoot
docs.text:
• Core functionality: Live classroom quizzes, fast join via PIN, real-time response aggregation, instant result display.
  • Why relevant to our project: Matches our live instructor-led flow and low-friction student entry.
  • What we will imitate:
  • PIN-based join flow and clear “session state” UX (waiting/open/closed).
  • Real-time response updates for instructor dashboard.
  • What we will not imitate:
  • Game-heavy scoring/leaderboards (not aligned with discussion-focused teaching).

docs.title: Jackbox Games
docs.text:
• Core functionality: Host-controlled room sessions, player join by room code, synchronized multi-user state.
  • Why relevant to our project: Strong model for host/student role separation and robust session control.
  • What we will imitate:
  • Instructor as authoritative host for opening/closing prompts.
  • Reliable code-based joining and reconnect behavior.
  • What we will not imitate:
  • Entertainment-focused UI mechanics not needed for academic use.

docs.title: NotebookLM
docs.text:
• Core functionality: Upload source materials, AI-grounded synthesis from those sources, structured outputs.
  • Why relevant to our project: Similar to our prompt generation from lecture files/transcripts.
  • What we will imitate:
  • Source-grounded AI prompting pipeline to reduce hallucinations.
  • Content-ingestion step before generation (file/transcript preprocessing).
  • What we will not imitate:
  • General-purpose note workspace features outside lesson flow.

docs.title: Cluely
docs.text:
• Core functionality: In-class participation prompts, engagement tracking, response collection.
  • Why relevant to our project: Similar educational context and participation goals.
  • What we will imitate:
  • Lightweight student response UI and engagement indicators.
  • Instructor-side visibility of participation trends.
  • What we will not imitate:
  • Features that require student identities (our design prioritizes anonymity).

docs.title: Open-source Projects
docs.text: • Open Notebook: We’re using Open Notebook as a technical reference since it’s a proven open-source system that already handles the complex challenge of processing multiple document types (PDFs, presentations, papers) and using AI to generate responses from that content, similar to Google’s NotebookLM. It has an MIT license.

docs.title: Accessibility & Style
docs.text: Our style guidelines prioritize legibility, clear contrast, and easily tappable interfaces.

docs.title: 1. Color & Contrast (Mandatory)
docs.text:
We cannot rely on color alone to convey meaning (e.g., a red background for an error must also have an error icon or clear text message).

  • The Contrast Rule: All text and critical UI elements MUST pass a WCAG AA contrast ratio (at least 4.5:1 for normal text, and 3:1 for large text).
  • The Tool: Before adding any new color combination to the UI, you must verify it passes using this Colour Contrast Checker.

docs.title: 2. Typography & Scale
docs.text:
Because we are designing for two drastic extremes (a massive projector screen vs. a 6-inch phone), text scaling is critical.

  • Instructor View (Projector/Desktop): Text must be large enough to be read from 30 feet away. Use heavy, high-contrast headings for the AI-generated prompts. Avoid ultra-thin font weights entirely.
  • Student View (Mobile): Base body text must be at least 16px.

docs.title: 3. Touch Targets & Interactivity
docs.text:
Students are often interacting with the app quickly, sometimes with one hand while taking notes.

  • Minimum Touch Area: Any clickable element (buttons, links, form inputs) must have a minimum touch target area of 45x45 pixels.
  • Safe Spacing: Provide ample padding between clickable elements to prevent "fat-finger" errors (e.g., accidentally hitting "Submit" instead of "Cancel").

docs.title: 4. Visual Hierarchy & Focus States
docs.text:
Instructors managing a live lecture might use keyboard navigation to quickly jump through the dashboard.

  • Focus Indicators: Never remove the default browser focus outline (outline: none;) for keyboard users unless you are replacing it with a custom, highly visible focus ring.
  • Action Colors: Reserve your primary, most vibrant color only for primary actions (like "Generate Prompt" or "Submit Answer"). Do not use the action color for static text or decorative backgrounds, as it confuses users about what is clickable.
